1:
The witnesses open the tragedy of Amnon and Tamar by reading it first as prophecy fulfilled. John Gill sets the timing: this comes 'after' the sin with Bathsheba, David's repentance, his pardon, and the birth of Solomon as a token of reconciliation — yet, he insists, the divine threatenings must still take effect, having begun already in the death of the child and now continuing here. Keil and Delitzsch draw the whole arc: the judgments announced to David through Nathan (2 Sam. 12:10) begin to fall upon his house, and they are brought about by his own sons' crimes — for which David is partly to blame, both by his want of paternal discipline and by the bad example he had set. His sons grew up without strict correction, under mothers jealous of one another, and so fancied they might gratify their own lusts; Amnon, the eldest, leads the way.

The grammatical-historical commentators settle the family facts. Ellicott notes that the formula 'after this' applies to the narrative as a whole, not to the mere fact that Absalom's sister was Tamar; both were children of Maacah, daughter of Talmai of Geshur, and the events probably followed soon after the Ammonite war and the marriage to Bathsheba. Adam Clarke fixes the relationships that will make the crime so vile: Tamar was the daughter of David and Maacah and the full sister of Absalom, but only the half-sister of Amnon, David's firstborn by Ahinoam.

The Puritan Matthew Henry draws the moral that governs the chapter. This is abominable wickedness, a subject not to be mentioned without blushing — and all the more so because a son of David does it. Godly parents have often been afflicted with wicked children, for 'grace does not run in the blood, but corruption does.' Amnon's lust is unnatural in itself, a craving after forbidden fruit that the more it is forbidden the more greedily it is desired; and beauty, a snare to many, was so to Tamar. Henry sees the devil at work as an unclean spirit putting the desire into Amnon's heart, and as a subtle serpent supplying the means. The voices do not contend here; together they read the opening verse as the hinge on which David's chastisement turns — the sword promised to his house (2 Sam. 12:10) beginning to move among his own children.
2:
The witnesses read Amnon's sickness as the self-inflicted torment of an unlawful passion. John Gill describes him as distressed, straitened, and perplexed in mind through the unruly and unbridled lusts that raged in him. The Wesleyan Adam Clarke sharpens the diagnosis with a borrowed observation from Delaney: the passion of love is nowhere so wasting and vexatious as where it is unlawful, for 'a quick sense of guilt strikes the soul with horror, and the impossibility of an innocent gratification loads that horror with desperation' — a conflict too cruel for human bearing. Amnon is thus already a portrait of lust punishing itself before ever it acts.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain why the sickness took hold. Ellicott corrects the English 'thought it hard' to 'it seemed impossible to Amnon': Tamar's modest seclusion in the harem of her mother left him, he supposed, no opportunity to carry out his desire, since the king's grown children lived in separate households. Keil and Delitzsch add that Amnon's miserable appearance was what first drew the notice of his cousin Jonadab, and note in passing that the Shimeah named here is the Shammah of 1 Sam. 16:9, David's brother.

The Wesleyan John Wesley gathers the point most briefly: she was a virgin, and therefore diligently kept, so that he could get no private converse with her. The barrier that guarded Tamar's chastity is precisely what inflames and frustrates Amnon, and the witnesses agree in the lesson it teaches — that lust, denied a lawful object and denied even the occasion of an unlawful one, does not subside but festers, wearing down the body it possesses. What looks at first like the pining of love is really, as the whole chapter will prove, the sickness of a will already given over to evil, waiting only for a crafty friend to show it the way. The barrier of Tamar's virtue and seclusion, meant for her protection, becomes in Amnon's diseased imagination merely an obstacle to be overcome, and his sickness the first fruit of a heart that has made an idol of forbidden desire. What the commentators will not allow is that this should be dignified with the name of love, for love seeks the good of its object, and Amnon seeks only himself.
3:
The witnesses fix on the sinister figure of Jonadab, the friend who is really an enemy. John Gill notes with grim brevity that though he is called a friend, his friendship in the issue proved Amnon's ruin. The Puritan Matthew Henry, treating the whole scene, presses the irony: Amnon had a friend, so called, but really a foe, a kinsman who had in him more of David's blood — for he was David's nephew — than of David's spirit; he was a subtle man, cunning to carry on any bad design, and especially an intrigue of this nature.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke is the most indignant. He calls Jonadab's counsel 'most diabolic advice,' and turns the verse into a rebuke of every nostalgia for a golden past: 'Say not thou, What is the cause that the former days were better than these?' Take the ancient times all together, Clarke says, and we may thank God they are past. The primitive world was no age of innocence when it could breed a Jonadab.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott does the careful philology and genealogy. The word rendered 'subtil,' he explains, indicates simply sagacity and wisdom, whether rightly or wrongly exercised — the same shrewdness that might have served a good end is here bent to a wicked one. He identifies Shimeah with the Shammah who appears as the third son of Jesse in 1 Sam. 16:9, and observes that this same house produced another son, Jonathan, who slew one of the giants (2 Sam. 21:21) — so that cleverness and courage alike ran in the family, though in Jonadab the cleverness went to serve a cousin's lust. The voices do not divide; together they warn that the most dangerous enemy is the flattering friend, and that gifts of mind, ungoverned by the fear of God, become the readiest instruments of another's sin. Where a true friend would have startled at the mention of such wickedness and laboured to divert Amnon from it, Jonadab lends his wit to contrive the very thing, and so shares the guilt of all that follows.
4:
The witnesses read Jonadab's probing question as the moment the trap is baited. Keil and Delitzsch render the taunt closely — 'Why art thou so wasting away, king's son, from morning to morning?' — and catch a physician's detail in it: the morning is named because sick persons look worst then, so the crafty friend seizes on the very hour when Amnon's pallor is most convincing. The word Gill and the Hebraists render 'lean' means wasting, spare, miserable in look, and Jonadab's flattering address to 'the king's son' plays deliberately on Amnon's pride of place.

The Puritan Matthew Henry expounds the poison in that title. To ask why the king's son should be lean is to remind him of two things — that he has the pleasures of the court to divert him and the power of a prince to command what he wants. Henry hears in it the whole philosophy of the abuse of greatness: 'Whatever is pleasing is lawful; your will is law.' It was the same reasoning Jezebel used on Ahab in a like case, 'Dost not thou govern Israel?' (1 Kings 21:7). The abuse of power, Henry warns, is the most dangerous temptation of the great, for it whispers that rank sets a man above the law of God.

The Reformed Gill and the Wesleyan voices note that Amnon, pressed, has the impudence to own his wicked lust and to miscall it love: 'I love Tamar, my brother Absalom's sister.' The very naming of her as Absalom's sister, the witnesses observe, betrays that Amnon knows the relation and the danger, and confesses his desire in spite of it. Henry adds the searching contrast that runs through his whole treatment: with far more reason may we ask dejected and disconsolate saints, who are children of the King of kings and heirs of the crown of life, why they are lean from day to day. The voices agree that Jonadab's question is not sympathy but seduction — a skilled hand drawing out the secret it means to exploit, and dignifying with the name of love a passion that Amnon himself, if honest, would have called by its true name.
5:
The witnesses examine Jonadab's plan and marvel at its cold ingenuity. John Gill calls him a subtle man who at once formed a scheme to relieve Amnon and open a way to the enjoyment of what he desired. Keil and Delitzsch spell out why the advice was so craftily devised: Amnon's wretched appearance would lend colour to the pretence of illness, and an affectionate father might be expected to gratify a sick son — the more so because the strange food-cravings of the ill are proverbial and hard to satisfy, so that even an odd request would not seem suspicious.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott presses the exact sense of the words: 'make thyself sick' should read 'feign thyself sick.' The point is deliberate, for the real pining of ungoverned passion has already been mentioned in v. 2; this now is a crafty feigning of sickness — yet the miserable condition produced by the former gives plausibility to the latter. The genuine sickness of lust supplies the disguise for the counterfeit sickness of the plot.

The Wesleyan John Wesley draws out the two small touches by which Jonadab makes the scheme safe. Amnon is to call Tamar 'my sister' — a word chosen, Wesley notes, precisely to prevent any suspicion of a dishonest design upon so near a relation; and he is to ask that she serve him 'at her hand,' pretending that his stomach was so nice he could eat nothing but what he saw dressed, and that by a person he particularly esteemed. Every detail is calculated to disarm David and to isolate Tamar. The witnesses do not differ in their judgment: this is not the improvisation of a moment but a practised cunning that knows how to use a father's tenderness, a sister's obedience, and the licence granted to the sick as the very machinery of a crime. That such a plan could be laid so smoothly is, to the commentators, the measure of how far both Amnon's heart and his counsellor's had already gone; the sin is conceived in full deliberation, and the appeal to natural affection is bent into a weapon against the innocent.
6:
The witnesses watch the plot pass from counsel into action as Amnon lays himself down and feigns sickness. John Gill notes simply that he took the advice of his cousin and acted upon it, while the grammatical-historical Ellicott observes how naturally the request fell out: that an invalid should wish to eat 'at her hand' seemed reasonable, and was readily granted. Ellicott clarifies the geography of the scene too, glossing 'sent home' as literally 'into the house' — that is, to the private apartments of the women, the harem, from which Tamar would be summoned.

The Puritan Matthew Henry dwells on David's part with a tenderness that turns to sorrow. David was always fond of his children and concerned if anything ailed them; no sooner does he hear that Amnon is sick than he comes himself to visit him. Let parents learn from this, Henry says, to be compassionate toward their children. Yet the same fatherly love is here the very hinge of the tragedy, for it walks straight into the snare. Henry supposes that David, at parting, even gave his sick son good counsel and prayed with him — which did not alter Amnon's wicked purpose in the least — and then asked whether there was anything he had a mind to. The dissembling son names the one thing that will serve his design: cakes from his sister Tamar's own hand, made where he can see them.

Henry adds the note that all the witnesses feel but he states most plainly: 'God hid his heart from understanding in this matter.' David saw no reason to suspect mischief and innocently gave the order — an order he afterwards, no doubt, reflected on with great regret. The commentators do not divide; they read the verse as a study in how genuine affection, when it is not joined to watchfulness, can be made the unwitting servant of another's sin. David's compassion is real and even commendable, and that is what makes the moment so bitter: the same providence that is executing the sentence on his house (2 Sam. 12:10) uses a father's love as the door through which the evil enters. Amnon's cunning has reckoned exactly on that love, and it does not fail him.
7:
The witnesses note how the king himself, all unknowing, becomes the instrument that delivers Tamar into danger. John Gill supposes that Tamar was not in the palace but at her brother Absalom's house, from which David now sends for her with the innocent command to go and dress food for her sick brother. Keil and Delitzsch simply lay the narrative out — David sends home to Tamar, Tamar goes to Amnon's house — and let the plain sequence carry its own weight of dramatic irony: the doting father is the one who orders the very meeting the schemer required.

The commentators feel keenly that David is here made an unwitting accomplice. The whole design of Jonadab, as the Wesleyan and Reformed voices have shown, turned on securing the king's own word to bring Tamar to Amnon alone; and now that word is given. What makes the moment so painful in the reading is that everything about it looks like ordinary kindness — a father caring for a sick son, a son wanting a familiar comfort, a sister summoned to a natural service. Not one of the actors, save Amnon, has any thought of evil, and that innocence is exactly what the plot has counted upon.

The witnesses read the verse against the larger providence that governs the chapter. David's earlier sin had been secret and calculated; here his very virtues — his tenderness, his readiness to help — are turned against him, so that he contributes with his own hand to the shame of his house. The commentators do not moralize the point into blame of David for this order, which he could not have known to be dangerous; rather they let it stand as an instance of how the threatened sword (2 Sam. 12:11) advances under cover of the most guiltless acts. Tamar, for her part, goes as innocently as she is sent, with no suspicion of a brother, still less of a sick brother; and the reader, who knows what she does not, feels the trap close. The single command of this short verse is thus the point on which the whole catastrophe turns, and the witnesses mark it as such.
8:
The witnesses linger on Tamar's simple, dutiful obedience as she comes to bake for the brother who means her harm. John Gill reads her going as an act both of obedience to the king's command and of affection toward her brother, undertaken 'with an innocent breast, having no suspicion of any design upon her chastity.' She kneads the flour and makes the cakes in Amnon's very sight, the openness of the act underscoring how far she is from imagining evil.

The Puritan Matthew Henry turns her homely industry into a small portrait of virtue. Though she was a king's daughter, a great beauty, and richly dressed, she did not think it below her to knead cakes and bake them — nor would she have done it so readily now, Henry infers, had she not been used to it. Good housewifery, he says, is not a thing beneath the greatest ladies, nor ought they to count it a disparagement; he points to the virtuous woman whose husband sits among the elders and who yet 'worketh willingly with her hands' (Prov. 31:13). Henry even draws a pastoral application: preparing for the sick should be more the care and delight of great ladies than preparing for the nice — charity more than curiosity.

The commentators feel the pathos of the scene precisely because Tamar's goodness is so unguarded. Every quality that adorns her — her obedience, her modesty, her willingness to serve, her freedom from suspicion — is exactly what leaves her exposed. The witnesses do not fault her for it; her innocence is no failing but a virtue, and the guilt lies wholly with the man who exploits it. Yet they mean the reader to feel the contrast between the pure heart that stoops to a menial kindness for a sick brother and the polluted heart that has arranged the whole occasion for a vile end. Tamar bakes in the open, in his sight, hiding nothing, while Amnon watches and waits for the moment to send the servants away. The house-wifely diligence that Henry commends becomes, in this setting, the last unshadowed act of Tamar's untouched life, and the witnesses record it with a tenderness that makes what follows the harder to read.
9:
The witnesses read the emptying of the room as the moment the trap is sprung. John Gill notes that Tamar poured out the cakes before Amnon so that he might see and know of what and in what manner they were made, that his stomach might not recoil — she is still playing the careful nurse. But he refused to eat, and then, with the caprice of a whimsical patient, ordered every man out from him. Keil and Delitzsch gloss the rare word for the pan or frying-pan and read Amnon's refusal and his command to clear the room as of a piece with the pretended illness — the indulged sick man's arbitrary whims giving cover to a darker purpose.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott presses exactly why no alarm was raised. Amnon's refusal to eat 'seemed natural enough in a whimsical invalid,' and 'for the same reason his next requirement, Have out all men from me, awakened no suspicion in the mind of Tamar.' The plot has been so contrived that each sinister step wears the face of a sick man's harmless fancy, and Tamar, reading his behaviour by her own innocence, sees nothing to fear in being left alone with a brother she has come to tend.

The Puritan Matthew Henry lifts the veil on the true reason for the emptied room, borrowing the words of Job: the adulterer — much more so vile an adulterer as this — 'is in care that no eye see him' (Job 24:15). The sick must be humoured, and think they have a privilege to command; so the servants are all turned out, and the last witness to Amnon's crime is removed. The commentators do not divide; together they mark the terrible economy of the scene, in which the ordinary licence granted to the sick becomes the instrument of an atrocity. What Tamar takes for the fretfulness of an invalid is in fact the deliberate clearing of the ground, and the very trust that made her serve him now leaves her defenceless. The room is emptied not for rest but for ruin, and the witnesses read the closing door as the hinge between her innocence and her wrong.
10:
The witnesses follow Tamar into the inner chamber and hear the mask fall. John Gill notes that Amnon calls for the meat to be brought into an inner chamber, at a greater distance, where they might be more secret and out of the reach of any of his household — the last precaution of a man who means to be neither seen nor overheard. Keil and Delitzsch carry the narrative to its turning point: when she handed him the food, he laid hold of her and said, 'Come, lie with me, my sister.'

The Wesleyan John Wesley reconstructs the movements with care. Amnon had been lying on his couch in the outer room where the company was and where Tamar made the cakes before him; he first sends all the men out of that room, and then rises from the couch and, on some pretence, withdraws into another, more secret chamber — drawing Tamar step by step from the open place of her service into the closed place of his crime. Each remove is a further stripping away of the safeguards that innocence had assumed were in place.

The commentators feel the awful contrast between the two who enter that room. Tamar comes still as a nurse, bringing food to a sick brother's bedside; Amnon waits as a predator who has arranged every circumstance. The word 'my sister,' which Jonadab had taught him to use in v. 5 precisely to disarm suspicion, is now turned into the language of his assault, the very name of kinship prostituted to lust. The witnesses read the inner chamber as the point of no return: the servants are gone, the door is near, the pretence of sickness has served its purpose, and nothing remains between Tamar and the wrong but her own words, which she will spend in the next verses. They do not moralize at length here; the horror of the moment is left to speak for itself, and the commentators simply mark how the whole apparatus of feigned illness, private request, and emptied room converges on this one seizure of an innocent woman by the brother she trusted.
11:
The witnesses read the seizure itself with restrained horror. John Gill observes that Tamar had brought the food not only into the chamber but to the very side of the bed or couch where Amnon had placed himself, so that she was within his reach at the moment he cast off all disguise. The whole elaborate pretence — the sickness, the cakes, the cleared room, the inner chamber — collapses into a single violent demand: 'Come, lie with me, my sister.'

The Puritan Matthew Henry catches the shock of the reversal. 'Now the mask is thrown off, the meat is thrown by,' he writes, and the wicked wretch who has just called her sister in the same breath courts her to lie with him. Henry counts it a base affront to her virtue that Amnon should think it even possible to persuade her, when he knew her behaviour to be always exemplarily modest and virtuous. It is common, he adds, for those who live in uncleanness to think others such as themselves — or at least ready tinder to their sparks — and Amnon's very expectation that Tamar might consent betrays how corrupt his own imagination had grown.

The commentators dwell on the word 'my sister' as the sharpest cruelty of the demand. The name that ought to have been Tamar's protection, the near relation that should have made the thought unthinkable, Amnon uses as if it were nothing, or worse, as if it lent his lust a kind of intimacy. The witnesses feel that the abruptness of the assault is itself part of its wickedness: there is no gradual overture, no attempt at persuasion first, but a sudden laying hold and a naked command, the act of a will that has long since decided and now simply seizes its moment. Tamar, brought to the bedside in trust, finds the trust answered by violence in the making. The verse is brief, and the witnesses keep their comment brief with it, letting the plainness of the record — a brother's hand upon a sister, a brother's mouth naming her sister as he demands her ruin — carry the full weight of the crime that is about to be attempted, and that Tamar will spend all her breath to prevent.
12:
The witnesses turn from Amnon's demand to Tamar's answer and find in it a model of chaste and reasoned resistance. John Gill hears her first word, 'Nay, my brother,' as itself a sufficient argument — he is her brother, she his sister, and that alone should forbid such an indignity. The Wesleyan Adam Clarke praises the speech as 'exceedingly tender and persuasive,' though he adds bitterly that 'Amnon was a mere brute, and it was all lost on him.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott trace the deep roots of her plea. Her words 'no such thing ought to be done in Israel' and 'do not thou this folly' deliberately recall Gen. 34:7, where the word 'folly' (nebalah) is first used of a want of chastity, in the defiling of Dinah. Such a sin, they insist, was utterly out of keeping with the calling and holiness of Israel, as the law itself declared (Lev. 20:8). Ellicott lays out the structure of her reasoning: she appeals first to what is forbidden in Israel though tolerated among the surrounding heathen, quoting the very words of the nation's tradition to recall the king's son to a sense of right; then she sets before him the personal consequences — the shame that would meet her everywhere and the ruin of his own name, so that he would be 'as one of the fools in Israel,' one who had cast off the fear of God and the restraints of decency.

The Wesleyan John Wesley gathers the force of each clause. 'My brother' names the relation that nature itself teaches him to abhor violating and obliges him to defend her from, even at his utmost hazard, should another attempt it. 'Do not force me' presses the added horror of violence: he ought to shrink from it even were she willing, but to add compulsion is abominable. And 'in Israel' invokes the people of God, taught better things, who will infinitely reproach so base an action. The witnesses are wholly united in their reading: Tamar's plea is not weak temporizing but a dense, principled appeal to kinship, to the law of God, to the honour of Israel, and to Amnon's own future — everything that reason and religion could urge against the crime. That it fails is no fault in the argument but only proof of how deaf lust had made its hearer.
13:
The witnesses read Tamar's continued pleading as reason straining every nerve against a settled violence. John Gill hears in 'whither shall I cause my shame to go?' a woman weighing her whole future: her reputation would be lost, so that even in the most private corner she would blush at the crime, and never could she expect to enter the married state — her character entirely ruined. Alongside her own disgrace she sets his: he would be 'as one of the fools in Israel,' a name of the deepest contempt.

The great interest of the witnesses gathers on her last suggestion — 'speak unto the king; for he will not withhold me from thee' — and here they divide over her intent. The Wesleyan Adam Clarke and John Wesley, with Gill, allow that she may really have supposed the king, rather than let his firstborn die for love, would dispense with the divine law and give her to Amnon in marriage; Clarke calls this 'another strong mark of indelicacy in those simple but barbarous times,' though he grants she may have said it only to divert him and escape his hands. The grammatical-historical Ellicott takes the more charitable and, he thinks, the more accurate view: the marriage of half-brother and sister was strictly forbidden in the Law (Lev. 18:9; Lev. 20:17), and it is not to be supposed that Tamar really thought David would violate its provisions; she made 'any and every suggestion to gain time and escape the pressing danger.' Amnon, Ellicott adds, knew the Law too well to hope for a legitimate marriage, and so persisted in his violence.

Keil and Delitzsch stand back from the question of her sincerity and simply mark the tragedy of the outcome: he would not listen to her voice, but, being stronger than she, forced her. The debate, then, is narrow and gentle — whether Tamar spoke in earnest hope of a royal dispensation or in a desperate bid to buy time — but on the substance the voices agree. Her every argument, honourable and shrewd alike, is spent in vain; and the very suggestion that might have delayed the crime only reveals how completely she is in the power of a man who has closed his ears to God, to Israel, and to her.
14:
The witnesses pass over the deed itself in a few grave words, letting its plainness carry its weight. John Gill notes only that Amnon 'would not hearken unto her voice,' his lust so inflamed and enraged that he could attend to no argument, however forcible. Keil and Delitzsch record the fact with the same restraint: he would not listen, but, being stronger than she, overpowered her, forced her, and lay with her. The brevity is deliberate; the commentators do not dwell where Scripture veils.

What the witnesses do draw out is the anatomy of the fall. Every restraint that God and nature had set about the act has now been broken through in turn — the tie of blood, the holiness of Israel, the plain word of the Law, the honour of the royal house, and the reasoned pleading of the victim herself. The Puritan Matthew Henry, surveying the whole scene, judges that a man could not arrive of a sudden at such a pitch of wickedness; he suspects that Amnon had long lived a lewd life, which his father either knew not or punished not, so that this atrocity was the ripe fruit of an unchecked course. His proud spirit could not bear a denial, and Tamar's comfort, honour, and all that was dear to her were sacrificed to his brutish and outrageous lust.

The commentators mark the collision of the two figures at its starkest here: on the one side chastity armed with every good reason, on the other appetite armed with mere strength. That strength alone should prevail is, to the witnesses, the very definition of the wrong — 'being stronger than she' is not a mitigation but the whole indictment, for it names a power used against right. Henry's earlier cry stands over the verse: 'God deliver all that are modest and virtuous from such wicked and unreasonable men.' The commentators do not moralize beyond what the record permits, but they let the reader feel that a threshold has been crossed which cannot be recrossed, and that from this violence the further evils of the chapter — Tamar's desolation, Absalom's hatred, and Amnon's own death — will all flow. The sword that Nathan foretold (2 Sam. 12:11) has drawn its first blood in the shame of a king's daughter.
15:
On the sudden turning of Amnon's love into hatred the witnesses reach one of their strongest agreements, and it is a theological one: this proves that what he felt was never love at all. Keil and Delitzsch state it as a settled principle — 'this sudden change furnishes a striking proof that lust is not love, but simply the gratification of the animal passions.' No sooner had Amnon gratified his passion than his supposed love turned into a hatred even greater than the love had been, so that he commanded the sister he had humbled to get up and be gone.

The other schools converge on the same insight from their own angles. John Gill traces the hatred to a stung conscience: having gratified his lust, Amnon could not bear the sight of the object that had been its instrument, and perhaps her sharp reproaches, calling him one of the fools in Israel, only sharpened his loathing. The grammatical-historical Ellicott quotes the pagan Tacitus, by way of Kirkpatrick, that 'it is characteristic of human nature to hate one whom you have injured,' and concludes with the rest that Amnon was governed not by love but by mere animal passion. The Wesleyan Adam Clarke confesses the conduct 'not only brutal but inexplicable,' owning that one may form conjectures about the cause but arrive at no certainty.

John Wesley adds the note the others imply but do not state — that this revulsion served a providential end. The hatred, he says, came 'by the just judgment of God both upon Amnon and David, that so the sin might be made public,' and way be made for the succeeding tragedies. What the sinner meant only as the recoil of a sated appetite, God overruled to bring the hidden crime into the open, where it would work its appointed chastisement on David's house. The witnesses thus read the verse on two levels at once: psychologically, as the unmasking of lust that had worn the name of love and now shows its true, self-hating face; and providentially, as the hinge by which a secret sin is turned toward public exposure and just recompense. On the first level they are unanimous, and it becomes the chapter's clearest moral — that the passion which will trample every law to possess its object holds that object in contempt the moment it is possessed.
16:
The witnesses read Tamar's last protest as a cry that the second wrong is worse than the first. John Gill gives her words their bare sense — 'there is no cause' for such treatment as this. Keil and Delitzsch wrestle with the difficult Hebrew and reach the meaning all follow: 'do not add to the great wrong thou hast done me the still greater one of thrusting me away.' Why greater? Because, they explain, to be cast out like this would make it appear that the shameful conduct had come from her, that she was the guilty party — whereas she was perfectly innocent, having done nothing but what affection to a sick brother dictated, and having been unable even to call for help (as the Law provided in Deut. 22:27) because Amnon had sent the servants away.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott, granting the clause elliptical and difficult, fixes on the moral logic: Amnon was now doing her a greater wrong than at first, 'because he was now bound, in consequence of that, to protect and comfort her.' Having ruined her, he owed her at the least the shelter of his house; to expel her was to pile cruelty upon violation and to publish her shame abroad.

The Wesleyan John Wesley draws the distinction with precision. The first act, he grants, was in itself the greater sin; but this expulsion was an act of greater cruelty and a greater calamity to her, because it exposed her to public infamy and contempt, and 'turned a private offence into a public scandal, to the great dishonour of God and of his people, and especially of all the royal family.' So the witnesses agree that Tamar's protest is neither confusion nor mere grief but a clear-eyed reckoning: a violated woman kept secretly might have hidden her wound, but a violated woman thrust into the street with the door bolted behind her is branded before all Israel as though the fault were hers. 'But he would not hearken unto her,' the verse ends — the same deafness that met her pleading before the deed now meets her pleading after it, and the witnesses mark that Amnon, who would hear no reason to spare her, will hear no reason to shield her either.
17:
The witnesses read the calling of the servant as the crowning insult, cruelty made deliberate and public. John Gill notes plainly that Amnon summoned the household servant who ministered to him. Keil and Delitzsch catch the studied contempt of the order: he commands the boy to 'put out this person' — no longer 'my sister,' but a mere 'this' — and to bolt the door behind her, so that the whole thing should wear the appearance of her having made some shameful proposal to him, and being justly turned away.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott presses the same point: 'Amnon doubtless intended to give the impression that Tamar had behaved shamefully towards him,' and 'the baseness of this insinuation is in keeping with his brutality.' The bolted door is not merely a dismissal but a slander acted out — a public staging designed to reverse the guilt, to make the victim seem the aggressor and the aggressor the wronged party.

The commentators feel that this calculated framing is, in its way, more revealing of Amnon's heart than the violence itself. The rape was the eruption of an ungoverned passion; this is the cold contrivance of a man who, having taken everything from his sister, now sets about robbing her of her good name as well, and arranges the very manner of her going so that the servants and the city will read it against her. The word he refuses her — the name of sister — is telling by its absence, for he who had called her sister when he wanted to disarm her (v. 11) will not own the relation now that he means to disgrace her. The witnesses do not divide; together they see in the bolted door the completion of Amnon's sin against Tamar, a threefold wrong of body, of shelter, and of reputation, each more deliberate than the last. And they note that the servant obeys without a word, so that the whole household is made complicit in the humiliation of a king's daughter, turned out like a thing of no account into the street where her tears will proclaim what her brother tried to conceal.
18:
The witnesses pause on the garment of divers colours, and find in that royal robe a silent aggravation of the crime. Keil and Delitzsch identify it as a long dress with sleeves, the ketoneth passim — the same phrase used of Joseph's coat in Gen. 37:3 — worn as a cloak by the virgin daughters of the king; and they draw the sharp conclusion: notwithstanding this dress, by which a king's daughter could at once be recognized, Amnon's servant treated Tamar like a common woman and turned her out of the house. The very badge of her royal virginity was on her as she was thrust into the street.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott confirms the philology: the word occurs only here and in the story of Joseph (Gen. 37:23), meaning a tunic with long sleeves in distinction from the short-sleeved kind commonly worn, and the fact is mentioned precisely 'to show that Tamar must have been recognised as a royal virgin' by the servant and by everyone else. Adam Clarke likewise refers the reader to the note on Joseph's coat, and John Wesley glosses it briefly as embroidered work.

The Reformed John Gill adds a note of his own antiquarian learning, canvassing whether the robe was woven of many colours, embroidered with figures, painted, or pieced together, and reporting Braunius's view that it was rather a sleeved garment reaching to the ankles and fringed at the borders. Gill draws one further edge from the custom of other nations: such flowered and many-coloured garments, he says, were in places like Athens the dress of harlots and not of virgins — an irony that hangs over the scene, for the robe that marked Tamar as an untouched princess is now the robe of a woman shamed. The witnesses do not contend over these details; together they let the garment do its work in the narrative. It is at once the token of what Tamar was — a king's virgin daughter, honoured and unmistakable — and the measure of what has been done to her, since the servant who could not have mistaken her rank yet handled her as a castoff. The evangelist's small notice of her dress, the commentators agree, is no idle description but a quiet witness to the enormity of the wrong.
19:
The witnesses read Tamar's mourning as a public proclamation of a wrong she had no power to punish. Keil and Delitzsch interpret each gesture: she took ashes upon her head and rent her sleeved dress as signs of grief and pain at the disgrace inflicted on her, and 'laid her hand upon her head' as a sign that a grievous trouble had come upon her, that the hand of God was resting as it were upon her — pointing to the like gesture in Jer. 2:37; and she 'went going and cried,' that is, crying aloud as she went along.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott renders the same clause literally, 'went going and cried,' and reads her open lament as both fitting in itself and prudent: Tamar put on every external mark of the deep grief within, and this was 'a proper means to obtain justice for her wrongs.' Her tears are not mere abandonment to sorrow but a testimony, laying her cause before whoever should see her.

The Wesleyan John Wesley draws out the meaning of each token in turn. The ashes signify her grief for the calamity that had befallen her; the hand upon the head is a mark of grief and shame, as if she were unable and ashamed to show her face; and the crying aloud was 'to manifest her abhorrency of the fact, and that it was not done by her consent.' This last point the witnesses feel to be the heart of it: a woman violated in secret and then cast out under a bolted door, made to look the guilty one, has only her open grief with which to declare her innocence, and Tamar spends it fully. John Gill adds simply that the ashes were the customary token of sorrow and distress. The commentators do not divide; together they see in the torn robe, the ashes, the covered head, and the loud weeping a single deliberate act of witness — the ruined princess turning her body into an accusation, so that the crime her brother tried to hide behind a bolted door is carried, weeping, through the open street for all Israel to see. What she cannot avenge she can at least refuse to hide, and in that refusal the witnesses find a mournful dignity.
20:
The witnesses read Absalom's meeting with his ruined sister as a mixture of tenderness and calculation. Keil and Delitzsch note that his very question — 'Hath Amnon thy brother been with thee?' — is a euphemism for what had happened, of the kind Scripture uses in Gen. 39:10, so that Absalom at once divines the truth. He quiets her: 'be silent; it is thy brother, do not take this thing to heart' — but he does so, they observe, because he was already determined on revenge and wished to conceal his plan for the time. So Tamar remained in his house 'desolate,' a word they render as laid waste, the joy of her life hopelessly destroyed.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads the scene the same way: Absalom sees at once how the case stands, comforts his sister, but counsels silence as necessary to the purpose of revenge he had immediately formed, and takes his desolate sister into his own home. John Gill supplies the plain setting, that Absalom either met her in the street or, more likely, spoke thus when she reached his house.

The Wesleyan John Wesley unfolds the double meaning packed into Absalom's counsel. 'Been with thee' he takes as an instance of 'the modesty of Scripture expressions,' worthy to be imitated. But the reasons Absalom urges for silence — he is thy brother, therefore forgive; thy disgracing him will be a blot to us all; thou wilt get no redress from David, since Amnon is as dear to him as thou; thy dishonour is the less because done by a king's son; this evil must be borne because it cannot be revenged — are, Wesley shows, so many veils drawn over his real design of taking vengeance at the first opportunity. The witnesses do not divide. Together they see a brother genuinely moved by his sister's wrong yet already bending that wrong to his own ends, comforting her with one hand while with the other he begins to nurse the hatred that will end in murder. Tamar is left desolate in his house, a living reproach kept before Absalom's eyes; and the commentators note the ominous quiet of the scene, in which nothing is said to Amnon and everything is felt.
21:
On David's mere anger the witnesses raise the chapter's sharpest pastoral question: why did the king not act? Keil and Delitzsch state the problem plainly. David heard all these things and became very wrathful, yet did nothing; the Law had enjoined that incest be punished with death (Lev. 20:17), and the Septuagint adds the telling clause, absent from the Hebrew, that David 'did not trouble the spirit of Amnon his son, because he loved him, for he was his first-born.' They will not alter the text on that authority, but they grant it probably gives the true reason for his inaction, together with David's own consciousness of guilt and his indulgent, undisciplined affection toward his sons — a weakness in his character that would bear bitter fruit.

The other schools converge on the same diagnosis. Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott both record the Septuagint addition, Clarke noting its presence also in the Vulgate and Josephus; Ellicott names David's 'guilty leniency,' and adds that 'the remembrance of his own sin also tended to withhold his hand from the administration of justice.' The Wesleyan John Wesley says David was wroth with Amnon 'whom yet he did not punish, at least so severely as he should,' whether from the consciousness of his own guilt in the like kind or from the foolish indulgence he often showed his children.

The Puritan Matthew Henry presses the point home with characteristic force. The beginning of this sin, like the beginning of strife, was 'as the letting forth of water'; David had reason enough to be wroth, that his own son should do such wickedness and draw him unwittingly to be accessory to it. But was it enough to be angry? He ought to have punished his son and put him to open shame, having power as both father and king to do it. Instead, Henry says, 'he fell into Eli's error, whose sons made themselves vile, and he frowned not on them' (compare 1 Sam. 3:13); his anger was made to serve instead of his justice, 'and this hardens sinners' (Eccl. 8:11). The witnesses are wholly united: David's unpunished wrath is the fatal hinge of the second half of the chapter, the failure of a father's justice that leaves the wrong to be avenged by a worse hand.
22:
The witnesses read Absalom's silence toward Amnon as the most dangerous kind of hatred — the kind that says nothing and forgets nothing. John Gill explains that Absalom said neither good nor bad to his brother about the rape; not that he sulked, for then Amnon would have mistrusted him and been on his guard, but on the contrary he talked freely and to all appearance friendly on other things, the better to conceal his hatred and his design to avenge his sister. John Wesley reads it the same way: Absalom neither debated the matter with Amnon nor threatened him, but seemed willing to pass it by with brotherly kindness — for had he wholly forborne all discourse it would have raised jealousies in Amnon and David alike.

The Puritan Matthew Henry gives the fullest anatomy of this concealed malice. Absalom hated Amnon, and 'he that hateth his brother is a murderer already,' who, like Cain, is of that wicked one (1 John 3:15; compare 1 John 3:12). His hatred of the crime would have been commendable, and he might justly have prosecuted it by due course of law; but to hate the person and design his death by assassination was to affront God, offering to repair the breach of the seventh commandment by the violation of the sixth, as if they were not all alike sacred — for he that said Do not commit adultery said also Do not kill (James 2:11).

Henry then names the marks that make this malice the worst of its kind. It is hidden closely and given no vent, whereas rebuking a neighbour to his face is set against hating him in the heart (Lev. 19:17); had Absalom reasoned the matter with Amnon he might have brought him to repentance, but by saying nothing he let Amnon's heart harden and his own grow more embittered. It is gilded over with a show of friendship, his words smoother than butter but war in his heart (Prov. 26:26). And it is harboured long — two full years he nursed this root of bitterness; for if the sun going down once upon wrath gives place to the devil (Eph. 4:26), what would the sunsets of two full years do? The witnesses stand together: Absalom's smooth silence is not forgiveness but murder in the making.
23:
The witnesses mark the two years' delay as both the measure of Absalom's malice and the proof of his cunning. John Gill notes that two complete years passed after the rape, so long did Absalom keep it in mind and contrive his revenge; and he let it lie so long precisely that the king, Amnon, and all the family might think it worn out of his mind and forgotten, and so confide in him the more. John Wesley reads the interval both ways at once: it is 'an aggravation of Absalom's malice, which was so implacable,' and at the same time 'an act of policy, that both Amnon and David might more securely comply with his desires.'

The grammatical-historical commentators fill in the occasion. Keil and Delitzsch explain that Absalom kept sheep-shearing — a festival celebrated with feasting, as at Nabal's shearing in 1 Sam. 25:2 — at Baal-hazor near Ephraim, where he must have held property; the exact site cannot now be determined. Adam Clarke adds that sheep-shearings were times when feasts were made and neighbours and relatives invited. Charles Ellicott gathers the strands: Absalom had silently nourished his revenge for two full years, and 'no doubt he chose also to give full opportunity for his father to punish Amnon's iniquity if he would'; by this long quiet waiting he so far disarmed suspicion that he was at last able to carry out his purpose.

The witnesses draw a common lesson from the calendar. A hatred that could wait two years without a word is more terrible than one that strikes in heat, for it is deliberate, patient, and wholly in command of itself. The very feast that should have been an occasion of family joy is chosen as the cover for a killing, and the sheep-shearing, a scene of rustic celebration, is turned into the setting of a fratricide. Ellicott's observation that the delay also gave David his chance to do justice sharpens the point already made at v. 21: had the father punished the first crime, the brother would have had no pretext for the second. The commentators do not divide; together they read the two silent years as the ripening of a settled purpose that only awaits its opportunity.
24:
The witnesses watch Absalom come in person to the king with an invitation that is really a stratagem. John Gill notes that he came to Jerusalem to invite David in person, presenting himself as the dutiful son honouring his father with the first fruits of his sheep-shearing. Keil and Delitzsch lay out the exchange that follows: Absalom pressed the king to come with all his servants, but David declined that he might not be burdensome, and though pressed would not go, but blessed him — wishing him, as at Nabal's feast, a pleasant and successful shearing (compare 1 Sam. 25:14).

The grammatical-historical Ellicott sees through the manoeuvre. Absalom, he says, 'could hardly have expected the king to accept his invitation, but by pressing him to go he effectively disguised his real purpose, and secured David's blessing.' The show of wanting his father present is the very thing that hides the design against his brother; and by extracting David's goodwill Absalom cloaks his plot in the appearance of family piety.

The Puritan Matthew Henry, treating the invitation in his running survey, reads it partly as ambition and display: Absalom invites the king and all the princes of the blood not only to pay his respects but that he might make himself the more respected among his neighbours, for those akin to great folks are apt to value themselves on their kindred. Yet Henry too senses the darker purpose gathering beneath the courtesy. The witnesses do not divide over the reading; together they see a scene of studied normalcy — a son's feast, a father's blessing, a family gathering — deliberately staged to lull every suspicion. The horror of it, for the commentators, lies in the contrast between the smiling occasion and the murder already commanded in Absalom's heart. Every gracious word he speaks to David is a thread in the net, and the king, who could not know it, is drawn once more, as at v. 7, into furthering his own sorrow. The invitation that looks like reconciliation is in truth the opening move of revenge, and the commentators read David's readiness to bless where he should have been wary as the mark of a heart that judged others by its own late-recovered penitence.
25:
The witnesses read David's polite refusal as the courtesy of a father who wishes not to burden his son — and, unknowingly, as the removal of the one obstacle Absalom needed removed. John Gill observes that David did not object to the invitation entirely; he was willing that some of the family should go, but not all. It seems probable, Gill infers, that the king particularly excepted himself and his eldest son, the heir to the crown — an exception that, as the next verses show, is exactly what Absalom's plan required him to overcome.

The Puritan Matthew Henry, in his continuous treatment, reads the refusal as prudent economy and turns it into a lesson for parents. The king would not go himself, Henry supposes, because he would not put Absalom to the expense of entertaining the whole court; for Absalom had an estate of his own on which he lived, given him by his father, but which the king would have him husband well. In this, Henry says, David is an example to parents: when their children are grown, to give them a competency to live upon according to their rank, and then to take care that they do not live above it, being no way accessory to their extravagance. It is prudent for young householders 'to begin as they can hold out, and not to spend the wool upon the shearing of it.'

The witnesses feel the quiet irony of the moment. David's very considerateness — his unwillingness to be a charge on his son, his wish to see Absalom manage his means wisely — is what leads him to excuse himself and so to open the door for the fatal request that Amnon go in his place. There is nothing culpable in the refusal itself; it is the act of a kind and sensible father. But it is precisely such innocent, well-meant decisions that the schemer has reckoned on and now turns to his purpose. The commentators do not moralize David here as they did at v. 21; rather they let the scene stand as another instance of how the tragedy advances through ordinary kindness, the king pressed and refusing, blessing and unaware, while his son manoeuvres him toward the very concession that will cost Amnon his life.
26:
The witnesses fasten on Absalom's crucial request — 'let my brother Amnon go with us' — and on David's hesitating question in reply. John Gill reads the opening 'if not' as Absalom's fallback: if the king will not himself do him the honour of coming, then let Amnon go. Keil and Delitzsch render it, 'And not — may my brother Amnon go with me?' The king would not at once consent; whether from suspicion, they say, cannot be determined with certainty, since he eventually yielded. From the length of time that had passed since the crime, without Absalom showing any wish for revenge, David 'might have felt quite sure that he had nothing more to fear' — for this long postponement of vengeance, that it might be carried out the more surely, is quite in the spirit of the East.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke explains why the request was pressed so plausibly: Absalom urged it 'because Amnon was the first-born, and presumptive heir to the kingdom,' and because he had disguised his resentment so well that he was not suspected. The very rank that should have guarded Amnon is made the pretext for singling him out. John Wesley agrees that David 'designed to keep him at home, as being his eldest son, and heir of his kingdom,' which is why Absalom had to name him particularly, and dresses the request in specious reasons — that since the king would not come, his heir might honour the feast in his stead, and that this would be a public token of friendship between the estranged brothers.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott catches David's dilemma exactly. The king hesitates, but 'as he could not well refuse without acknowledging a suspicion which he was unwilling to express, he finally consents.' Here the witnesses locate the psychological pivot of the whole plot. To deny Absalom would be to voice a distrust David either did not feel or would not own; to consent is to hand his firstborn to the killer. The commentators do not finally divide over whether David felt a flicker of misgiving — Keil and Delitzsch leave it open, Ellicott thinks a suppressed suspicion is just what trapped him — but they agree on the outcome: courtesy, and the reluctance to accuse without proof, disarm the father a second time, and the fatal permission is drawn from him.
27:
The witnesses read David's final yielding as the moment when human prudence is overruled by a higher purpose. John Gill observes that Absalom pressed him — which, one would think, should have increased any suspicion — but David's mind was blinded, 'that Amnon's incest might be punished, and the threatening to David and his house be fulfilled on account of the affair of Uriah and Bathsheba.' The very failure of the king's discernment is read providentially, as the means by which the sentence of 2 Sam. 12:10 is carried out.

The Wesleyan John Wesley states the same conviction even more directly: 'It is strange that his urgent desire of Amnon's company raised no suspicion in so wise a king; but God suffered him to be blinded, that he might execute his judgments upon David, and bring upon Amnon the just punishment of his lewdness.' Two ends are served at once — the chastisement of the father and the recompense of the son — and both through the ordinary channel of a permission reluctantly given.

The grammatical-historical commentators keep to the record and its curiosities. Keil and Delitzsch note that Absalom pressed him until he let Amnon and all the king's sons go, and observe that the Septuagint adds a gloss at the end of the verse, 'And Absalom made a feast like the feast of a king' — which Charles Ellicott also records as an explanatory addition to the Hebrew. The plainness of the narrative, with the arrangement of the meal passed over as subordinate, only heightens for the commentators the sense that everything now moves swiftly toward the appointed end. The witnesses converge on a single reading: that David's consent, wrung from him against his own inclination to keep his heir at home, is at once a human failure of watchfulness and a link in the chain of divine justice. They do not make God the author of Amnon's murder, which is wholly Absalom's crime; but they see the withdrawal of David's ordinary caution as the permission by which the long-threatened sword is at last let loose within his house, so that the king who would not do justice on Amnon is made, once again as at v. 7, the unwitting instrument that sends him to his death.
28:
The witnesses read Absalom's charge to his servants as cold-blooded murder dressed in the forms of command. John Gill notes that Absalom had given these orders beforehand, before the guests sat down: they were to mark when Amnon's heart was merry with wine, and at his word 'Smite Amnon,' to kill him and fear not. The Wesleyan John Wesley draws out the calculation in the timing and the promise: Amnon is to be struck when merry, that is, when he least suspects and is least able to prevent the blow; and the 'I' who commands — 'have not I commanded you?' — is the king's son and, once Amnon is gone, his heir, who therefore can easily obtain the servants' pardon and richly reward them.

The Puritan Matthew Henry gathers the aggravations of the sin one by one. Absalom would have Amnon slain when his heart was merry with wine, least apprehensive of danger, least able to resist, and least fit to go out of the world — as if his malice aimed to destroy both soul and body, giving him no time to say, Lord, have mercy upon me. He employs his servants to do it, involving them in the guilt; and he bids them defy the express command of God, 'Thou shalt not kill,' upon no warrant but his own — 'Have not I commanded you? that is enough.' Those servants, Henry warns, are ill-taught who obey their masters in contradiction to God, and those are wicked masters who teach them so, for their big words cannot secure them from God's wrath.

Henry adds two further edges the other witnesses feel. The murder was to be done in the presence of all the king's sons, who were chief rulers (compare 2 Sam. 8:18), so that it was an affront to public justice and to the king their father whom they represented. And there is reason to suspect that Absalom did this not only to avenge his sister but to make way for himself to the throne, since with Amnon the eldest taken off he would stand next in line. The witnesses do not divide; together they see in the quiet order given over the wine cup a premeditated fratricide that tramples the sixth commandment, corrupts the servants who execute it, and reaches, beyond revenge, toward a crown.
29:
The witnesses record the deed and its immediate aftermath as the visible fulfilment of Nathan's prophecy. John Gill notes simply that the servants smote and killed Amnon when Absalom gave the word. Keil and Delitzsch add that thereupon all the other king's sons arose, and every man mounted his mule and fled, scattering in terror from the feast that had turned to slaughter.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott fills in the circumstances. The servants obeyed without question, for it was quite customary for a prince's retainers to carry out his orders and leave the whole responsibility with him. If Chileab, David's second son, was already dead, as seems probable, then Absalom now stood next in the succession after Amnon, and his men may well have looked on the killing as a step toward the throne. The blow was too sudden and unexpected to allow the other princes to interfere. Ellicott lingers even over the mules on which they fled: though David had reserved horses from his Syrian spoils (2 Sam. 8:4), the mule was still the mount of persons of distinction (compare 1 Kings 1:33), even though the breeding of mules had been forbidden in the Law (Lev. 19:19) and they were brought in only by commerce.

The Wesleyan John Wesley sounds the note that unites the witnesses: 'Now the threatened sword is drawn in David's house, which will not depart from it.' The sentence spoken by Nathan (2 Sam. 12:10) has begun to fall; David's eldest son falls by that sword through his own wickedness, and his father, by his connivance at the first crime, is accessory to the second. The commentators hold the two truths together without strain. Amnon dies justly, reaping what his lust had sown; Absalom kills wickedly, a murderer whatever the provocation; and over both, the word of God against David's house works itself out, so that the king's failure to punish incest returns upon him as the murder of his heir. The scattering princes on their mules carry the news of a judgment that David himself had set in motion two years before by his own guilty silence.
30:
The witnesses turn to the false report that outran the truth, and find in it a study of rumour and of grief. Keil and Delitzsch note that while the king's sons were still on the road, tidings reached David, and, as generally happens in such cases, with very great exaggeration: 'Absalom hath slain all the king's sons, and there is not one of them left.' The Puritan Matthew Henry makes the observation general: 'It is common for fame to make bad worse; and the first news of such a thing represents it as more dreadful than afterwards it proves.' Let us not therefore be afraid of evil tidings while they want confirmation, he counsels, but when we hear the worst, hope the best, or at least hope better.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke delights to enlarge the point with classical learning. 'Fame never lessens but always magnifies a fact,' he writes; 'Report, contrary to the nature of all other things, gains strength by going.' He quotes Virgil's famous personification of Rumour from the Aeneid — Fama, the swift plague that grows with movement and gathers vigour as she goes — as the very image of what befell this news on its way to Jerusalem. The grammatical-historical Ellicott, characteristically, turns the same feature to a question of authorship: 'the story of this exaggerated report, so true to the life, indicates contemporaneous authorship' — the vivid accuracy of the panic being evidence that the narrative comes from an eyewitness age.

Yet the witnesses do not let the point about rumour eclipse the reality of David's suffering. Henry observes that the false news gave the king as much affliction, for the present, as if it had been true: he tore his garments and lay on the earth while as yet it was only a flying story. 'It was well that David had grace,' Henry adds, 'he had need enough of it, for he had strong passions.' The commentators thus read the verse on two levels — as a lesson not to be crushed by unconfirmed evil tidings, and as a glimpse of a father's heart already breaking under a grief that, though not so wide as reported, was real and deserved enough. The exaggeration would soon be corrected; the sorrow would not, for Amnon indeed was dead.
31:
The witnesses read the king's violent mourning as the outward sign of a grief that seemed, for the moment, total. John Gill compares David's tearing of his garments to Jacob's when he was shown the coat of Joseph and supposed him slain (compare Gen. 37:34) — for David, believing the exaggerated report, thought all his sons were dead. Keil and Delitzsch paint the scene closely: the king rent his clothes with horror at such a deed and sat down upon the ground, while all his servants, his courtiers, stood motionless by, their own clothes rent as well; they note that the Hebrew word rendered 'stood' often carries the sense of standing perfectly still (as in Num. 22:23), a picture of a whole court frozen in shock.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott attends to the words themselves, observing that 'tare his garments' should read 'rent his clothes,' the same expression as in the last clause of the verse, so that king and servants alike perform the identical act of grief. The repetition binds the sovereign and his household in one gesture of horror, the court mirroring the king.

The witnesses feel the poignancy that this deepest grief rested, at this instant, on a falsehood — David mourns as bereaved of all his sons when only one is dead. Yet the commentators do not treat his sorrow as wasted or foolish, for the horror was just: a son murdered by a son, at a brother's feast, is cause enough for a king and his court to sit in the dust. The tearing of the royal robes recalls, without the witnesses labouring it, the earlier robe of this very chapter — the torn garment of divers colours in which Tamar had mourned her own ruin (v. 19) — so that father and daughter alike are clothed in the tokens of a shame and grief that Amnon's lust first set in motion. The commentators leave the scene in its stillness: a king on the earth, a court standing rent and silent, waiting in silence for the truth that Jonadab is about to speak.
32:
The witnesses return to Jonadab, now reappearing to correct the report he was perhaps best placed to have prevented. John Gill and Adam Clarke both identify him as the very man who had advised Amnon to feign sickness and so contrived the rape; Clarke marvels that this 'very bad man' now 'speaks coolly of a most bloody tragedy, which himself had contrived.' Keil and Delitzsch render his reassurance — let not the king suppose all the young men are slain, for Amnon only is dead — and explain his eerie certainty: it was 'laid upon the mouth of Absalom,' a thing determined, from the day that Amnon forced his sister Tamar. Jonadab, in other words, had read Absalom's fixed purpose in his very speech and silence.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott presses the same point: this 'subtle counsellor who had led Amnon into his sin now at once divined how the case really stood and reassured the king,' rendering the difficult clause 'upon Absalom's mouth it hath been set.' The man's cleverness, which had served lust before, now serves as an uncanny insight into another's revenge.

The Puritan Matthew Henry turns Jonadab's knowledge into an indictment. If he could tell the king so confidently that Amnon alone was dead, and that the deed had been designed by Absalom from the day of the rape, then what a wicked man he was not to have warned David sooner — that means might have been used to make up the quarrel, or at least that Amnon might not have been let go to Absalom's house. 'If we do not our utmost to prevent mischief, we make ourselves accessory to it'; and Henry appeals to the searcher of hearts who ponders whether we truly did not know (compare Prov. 24:12). It may be, he concludes, that Jonadab was as guilty of Amnon's death as he had been of his sin — the false friend who would not prevent the crime proving equally willing not to prevent the ruin. The witnesses converge: Jonadab's calm accuracy is not innocence but the composure of a man who had known too much all along, and whose knowledge, kept to himself, made him a partner in both the sin and its bloody recompense.
33:
This brief verse, in which Jonadab presses his reassurance home, the witnesses treat as the completing of the correction begun in the verse before. John Gill gives its plain sense: let not the king take the thing to heart, neither supposing nor being troubled that all the king's sons are dead, for Amnon only is dead. Jonadab urges David not to let his imagination run beyond the fact, and to compose his grief to the true, smaller, though still bitter, measure of the loss.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch place the verse within the structure of the narrative, noting that the whole passage from the arrival of the report to this point (vv. 30–33) forms a kind of parenthesis, in which the writer pauses the action to describe the impression made upon the king and his court before the sons themselves arrive. The effect is to hold the reader, with David, in the suspense of a grief not yet rightly informed, until the watchman's sighting and the princes' coming resolve it.

The witnesses observe the moral discomfort of the scene without labouring it. It is Jonadab, of all men, who now plays the comforter, steadying the king with true information — and the irony is not lost on the commentators, who have just marked (at v. 32) that this same counsellor bore no small share in bringing the whole calamity about. His words are accurate and even soothing, yet they come from a mouth that had helped set the tragedy in motion, so that the reader receives the comfort with a measure of unease. The commentators do not divide over so plain a verse; together they read it as the quieting of David's worst fear, the narrowing of the reported slaughter of all his sons down to the real death of one. And they let the pause do its work: the king, told that Amnon alone is dead, is left to gather himself for the grief that remains true — the murder of his firstborn by his own son — while the eye of the narrative turns, in the next verse, to the watchman on the wall and the dust of the returning princes.
34:
The witnesses read this verse as two movements at once — Absalom's flight from justice and the watchman's first sighting of the returning princes. John Gill observes that Absalom, who had promised his servants protection, could not protect himself; he knew that what he had done was death by the Law, that there was no city of refuge for such wilful murder, and that he had no reason to hope David would let so foul a crime pass unpunished. Keil and Delitzsch note that the statement 'Absalom fled' follows directly on v. 29, the intervening verses being the parenthesis about the court; when the king's sons fled upon their mules, Absalom too took flight, but by another way.

The Puritan Matthew Henry draws the irony of the reversal. Absalom was now as much afraid of the king's sons as they had been of him: they fled from his malice, he from their justice. No part of the land of Israel could shelter him, for 'the cities of refuge gave no protection to a wilful murderer'; though David had let Amnon's incest go unpunished, Absalom could not promise himself pardon for this murder, so express was the Law and so well known was David's dread of blood-guiltiness. He therefore made the best of his way to his mother's relations.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott ties the threads together and adds a touch of local colour. This flight, he notes, is connected on the one side with the killing in v. 29 and on the other with the retreat to Geshur in v. 37; several things were happening at once, and the reason for mentioning Absalom's escape just here is that otherwise he would seem to be numbered among 'the king's sons' who came weeping. The watchman saw much people coming 'by the way of the hill side behind him' — that is, Ellicott explains, from the west, since the Oriental is always supposed to face the east when speaking of the points of the compass. The witnesses do not divide; together they set the guilty brother fleeing beyond Israel's borders while the innocent brothers come home, and mark that the murderer's own conscience, reading the Law aright where his father would not enforce it, drives him into exile.
35:
The witnesses read this verse as the confirming of Jonadab's word and the coming of grief in its true measure. John Gill notes that as the approaching company drew nearer it was plainly discovered that they were the king's sons, seen on the side of the hill; what the watchman had first spied as a crowd now resolves into the returning princes, and Jonadab's confident prediction — Amnon only is dead — is vindicated before the king's eyes.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the scene forward: Jonadab had hardly said 'Behold, the king's sons come; as thy servant said, so it is,' when the king's sons themselves arrived and lifted up their voice and wept, whereupon the king also and all his servants wept very sore. The counsellor's accuracy is complete, and the arrival turns the court's frozen horror into loud lamentation.

The witnesses feel the strange doubleness of the moment. On the one hand it is a relief, for the worst report is proved false and most of the sons are alive; on the other it is the beginning of the true mourning, for Amnon indeed is dead, and dead by a brother's hand. The weeping that breaks out is thus both a release from the greater terror and an entrance into the real grief. The commentators note that Jonadab stands vindicated as a prophet of the event — a bitter distinction, since his foreknowledge, as they have already observed at v. 32, argues how much he had understood of Absalom's purpose all along. The scene is one the witnesses do not need to expand: a watchman's cry, a counsellor's confirmed word, and then the princes cresting the hill and the whole court dissolving in tears. David, who a moment before had believed himself bereft of every son, now receives them back all but one, and learns in the same hour both that he has less cause for grief than he feared and that the grief he has is real, deserved, and the fruit of his own unpunished house. The commentators let the tears speak, and pass on with the narrative to the fuller notice of Absalom's flight.
36:
This short verse, recording the arrival and the weeping, the witnesses read as the resolution of the suspense that has hung over the court since the false report came. John Gill marks the timing precisely: as soon as Jonadab had made an end of speaking — the words scarcely out of his mouth — the king's sons came, and lifted up their voice and wept, and the king and all his servants wept very sore. The prediction and its fulfilment are laid side by side, so that word and event meet in the same instant.

The grammatical-historical reading, carried by Keil and Delitzsch across these verses, sees in the exact coincidence of Jonadab's speech and the princes' appearance a mark of the narrative's vividness and order: the counsellor had barely finished when the thing he foretold stood before them, and the pent grief of the court, held back through the parenthesis of vv. 30–33, now breaks out in a great and general weeping.

The witnesses do not divide over so plain a record, and its very plainness is part of its power. The whole company weeps together — the returned sons for the brother slain among them, the king for his firstborn, the servants with their master — a shared lamentation that gathers up the horror of the feast turned to bloodshed. The commentators feel the completeness of the reversal since the chapter's opening: the family that began with a secret lust now stands united only in open grief, the house of David weeping over a murdered heir. There is no comfort offered in the verse, only the honest sound of sorrow; and the witnesses let it stand as such, a moment of undivided mourning before the narrative turns to follow the one son who is not there — Absalom, already fled toward Geshur. The grief is real and reasonable, for, as the commentators have said elsewhere, sorrow is never more fitting than when there is sin in the case; and here sin of every kind — lust, injustice, hatred, and murder — has done its work, leaving the king and his house nothing to do, for the present, but weep very sore.
37:
The witnesses take up the notice of Absalom's flight to Geshur and the king's continual mourning. John Gill observes that the flight is repeated here 'for the sake of what follows,' the narrative circling back to Absalom in order to trace his refuge. Keil and Delitzsch read the clause as a circumstantial parenthesis inserted to define 'the king's sons' more exactly — only Absalom had fled — and note that Talmai the son of Ammihud, king of Geshur, was the father of Maacah, Absalom's own mother (2 Sam. 3:3); the murderer flees, that is, to his maternal grandfather's protection beyond Israel's border.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke and John Wesley agree on both the fact and its motive. Clarke explains that, having committed wilful murder, Absalom could not avail himself of a city of refuge and was obliged to leave the land of Israel altogether, taking shelter with Talmai his grandfather by the mother's side (compare 2 Sam. 3:3). Wesley adds the double purpose: Absalom went to his mother's father 'that he might have present protection and sustenance from him, and that by his mediation he might obtain his father's pardon' — flight and diplomacy in one.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch settle a small crux over whom David mourned. The verse says the king mourned 'for his son every day,' and Ellicott is clear that Amnon is certainly the son meant, the murdered firstborn for whom David continually grieved until time gradually assuaged his sorrow; Keil and Delitzsch take the subject 'David' as easily supplied from the preceding mention of the king. The witnesses thus read the verse as holding two figures in a single frame: the guilty son safe in a foreign court, and the grieving father at home, mourning the son he has lost while the son who killed him lives on out of reach. The commentators feel the sad symmetry — David neither able to recall his grief for Amnon nor yet moved to fetch home Absalom — and let it prepare for the strange longing that the closing verse will name, when the father's heart begins to turn, against all justice, toward the exile.
38:
The witnesses note that Absalom's flight is now recorded a third time, and ask why. John Gill reports the explanation of the Jewish commentator Abarbinel: when Absalom first fled to his grandfather he used to stand openly in the court of the palace and go about freely with Talmai; but when he learned how bitterly his father mourned Amnon's death, he grew afraid that David would send to seize him and take vengeance, and so he went no more abroad but abode continually in Geshur, which was a fortified city — hence 'was there three years.' The repetition, on this reading, marks a change in Absalom's manner of exile, from open residence to wary confinement.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott gives the fuller rationale for the threefold mention. This is the third time the flight has been named, he observes, but 'after the custom of Scripture narrative, each repetition has been for the purpose of introducing some additional fact.' In v. 34 the simple fact of his flight is stated; in v. 37 it is added that he went to his grandfather; and here that he remained with him three years. Keil and Delitzsch read the verse the same way, and connect it forward to the closing statement about David's changing heart.

The witnesses draw from the verse a sense of time suspended. Three years Absalom dwells in Geshur, neither pursued nor pardoned, while David, as Keil and Delitzsch note, gradually finds his grief for Amnon diminishing as the seasons pass. The exile is real and prolonged, a settled fact of the family's life, and the commentators feel in the bare notice of its length the slow work of time upon a father's sorrow and a son's estrangement. Neither party moves: David does not demand Absalom back, and Talmai does not think himself bound to send him unless he is demanded. The witnesses read the three years, therefore, as the pause in which the wound half-heals and the stage is set for the reconciliation that Joab will later contrive — the interval named here so that the reader may feel its length before the final verse discloses the turning of David's heart.
39:
On the chapter's last verse the witnesses openly divide, and the division turns on a single obscure Hebrew clause: did David's soul long for Absalom, or did David cease to go out against him? The received English, followed by John Gill, reads that 'the soul of King David longed to go forth unto Absalom.' Gill notes the verb is feminine and canvasses the Jewish attempts to construe it — Aben Ezra supplying 'the wife' (Absalom's mother interceding), Abarbinel holding that for three years David went out to seek vengeance until a woman restrained him — but he lets the plain sense stand: the king, comforted concerning Amnon now that he was dead, yearned after his surviving son. Adam Clarke and John Wesley read it the same way and lament it: Clarke sees a 'very strong paternal affection' for a young man with little to commend him but beauty, on which Joab would soon work to procure his return — adding that it would have been well for all had Absalom ended his days at Geshur, since his return only 'brought increasing wretchedness to his unfortunate father.' Wesley cries out at 'the amazing weakness' of a David who could not find it in his heart to do justice on the ravisher of his sister, yet could almost find it in his heart to receive into favour the murderer of his brother — the very sin of Eli, who honoured his sons above God.

Against this stands the grammatical-historical reading. Ellicott objects that 'the soul of' is not in the original, that the verb never elsewhere bears the sense given it, and that the picture is contrary to fact, since David could easily have recalled Absalom had he wished, yet afterward refused to see him (2 Sam. 14:24). He prefers to take the verb impersonally: 'David desisted from going forth against Absalom.' The king ought to have arrested and punished a murder that was at once fratricide and high treason, the assassination of the heir-apparent; but the flight to Geshur made this difficult, and as time went by 'David was comforted concerning Amnon' and gradually gave up the thought of punishing Absalom. Keil and Delitzsch reach the same construction, taking the verb in the sense of restraining: Absalom's flight and stay in Geshur held David back from going out against him, while his grief for Amnon steadily diminished.

Yet beneath the genuine grammatical dispute the witnesses land on one pastoral judgment. Whether David pined for his son in weak affection or merely let go the demands of justice, the verse closes the tragedy on the same note of a father undone by love of his children — the man who would not punish incest and cannot now punish murder, whose failure of discipline, foretold by Nathan, has cost him one son slain and another exiled, and whose heart, softening toward the guilty, is already preparing the way for the greater sorrows that Absalom's return will bring.
